title: React Native Memoization Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of React.memo, useMemo, useCallback, and performance optimization patterns.

questions.question: When does React.memo prevent a component from re-rendering?

options.text: When the component's internal state changes
When the shallow comparison of props shows they are identical to the previous render
When the component is not visible on screen
When the component has no children

questions.question: Why should you use useCallback when passing a function as a prop to a React.memo component?

options.text: useCallback makes the function execute faster
Functions defined inside a component get a new reference on every render; useCallback returns a stable reference so React.memo's comparison correctly identifies no change
useCallback is required by React.memo — it won't work without it
useCallback prevents the function from running more than once

questions.question: Why does passing `style={{ margin: 10 }}` inline break React.memo optimization?

options.text: Inline styles are not supported in React Native
StyleSheet.create is required for performance and disables inline styles
A new object literal is created on every render, so the reference always changes, causing React.memo to always see 'new' props
The margin property is not recognized by React Native's shallow comparison
A memoized callback function that doesn't change reference between renders
The cached result of a computation, recomputed only when specified dependencies change
A ref object that persists across renders without causing re-renders
A higher-order component that wraps children with memoization

questions.question: When is memoization NOT beneficial and should be avoided?

options.text: When the component renders a large list of items
For simple, fast-rendering components where the memoization comparison overhead exceeds the render cost itself
When the component receives string props
When using TypeScript — memoization is only supported in plain JavaScript
